title: Punch-needle wall piece
subtitle: A 30 x 30 cm framed punch-needle wall hanging on monks cloth, mounted in an embroidery hoop
excerpt: A 30 x 30 cm punch-needle wall piece worked on monks cloth stretched in a rug frame. Acrylic or wool yarn is worked from the back of the cloth; loops appear on the front face as the pile. A geometric or abstract botanical motif uses three to four colours. After finishing, the work is backed with felt sealed with fabric glue to lock the loops, then mounted in a 25 cm embroidery hoop for display.
sourceNotes: Punch-needle on monks cloth is a 20th-century North American development of traditional hooked-rug technique. The contemporary wall-hanging format (finished in a display hoop) is a modern adaptation without a single canonical source. Technique substance draws from standard UK and North American guild instruction. No named modern author's prose is quoted.

glossaryTerms.definition: A coarse-weave cotton backing fabric used for punch-needle and hooked rug work. The open, even weave allows the punch-needle barrel to pass through cleanly on every stroke and grips each formed loop securely. Standard monks cloth runs at approximately 7 to 8 warp ends per 5 cm; it is sold by the metre at approximately 150 cm wide.

The raised surface of a rug or textile formed by loops or cut ends of yarn or fabric strip standing perpendicular to the backing cloth. In punch-needle work, pile forms on the front face of the backing as loops pushed through from the back. Pile height is set by the depth-stop on the punch-needle handle.

A pile surface in which the yarn loops remain uncut, forming rounded arches that stand proud of the backing. Loop pile is the standard result of punch-needle work. It is more durable than cut pile for floor rugs because there are no exposed fibre ends to abrade; in wall pieces it produces a textured, slightly velvet-like surface.

content.text: Punch-needle wall pieces are worked from the back of
monks cloth
: the needle pushes through the cloth from behind, and the
loop pile

builds up on the front face without the maker seeing it directly during work. The finished design is flipped over and mounted in a display hoop. This front-back inversion means the drawn design on the back must be mirrored if any element is asymmetric. This piece uses a geometric or abstract botanical motif: a stylised leaf with a central spine and two flanking colour fields, or a diamond grid with alternating fill colours. Both read clearly at 30 x 30 cm and use only straight-run rows of
pile
, with no freehand curves to navigate.

Use DK or worsted-weight acrylic or wool yarn. Acrylic is more forgiving of tension variation and does not felt under the slight friction of repeated needle passes; wool gives a richer pile surface but requires a slightly larger needle eye. Allow approximately 25 to 30 m per colour for a design area of this size.
What you need

items.name: Monks cloth, 7 to 8 ends per 5 cm
items.qty: 50 x 50 cm (allows 10 cm overhang all around the 30 x 30 cm design area)

items.name: DK or worsted-weight wool or acrylic yarn, main colour
DK or worsted yarn, colour 3 (optional for botanical detail)
Punch needle (rug-making, hollow barrel), set to medium pile depth
Rug frame or gripper-strip frame to stretch monks cloth
Embroidery hoop, 25 cm (10 inch) for final display mounting
Rug binding tape or felt, approx 35 x 35 cm
items.qty: 1 piece felt (to back the finished piece)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the backing and transfer the design

Cut the monks cloth to 50 x 50 cm. Fold and press the raw edges back by 1.5 cm on all four sides to stop fraying. Mount the cloth on the rug frame with the back face upward: this is the face you will draw on and work from. The frame should hold the cloth drum-taut in both directions; a slack backing causes uneven pile and loops that pull free.

Draw the 30 x 30 cm design area on the back face with a permanent marker, following the grain of the weave. Then draw your motif inside that area. For a botanical leaf: draw a central vertical spine from top to bottom, then two mirror-image flanking areas on either side. For a diamond grid: divide the 30 x 30 area into a grid of 6 x 6 diamonds at 5 cm intervals. If the motif is asymmetric, mirror it left-to-right before drawing, as it will be reversed when displayed from the front.
Step 2: Thread the punch needle and set the pile depth

Thread the yarn through the hollow barrel of the punch needle from the handle end using the fine wire threading tool supplied with the needle, or a folded length of fine wire. The yarn should emerge from the open slot at the tip. Pull 6 to 8 cm of yarn through the tip before starting, so the first loop has enough tail to hold when the needle first pushes through.

Set the needle's depth stop to the medium pile setting: typically around 9 to 10 mm below the base of the handle grip. At this depth, loops on the front face will stand at roughly 8 to 9 mm, giving a balanced pile that photographs well and holds its shape in a hanging. Shallower settings produce a flatter pile closer to the cloth; deeper settings create a more shaggy surface.
Step 3: Work the pile from the back

Hold the hoop with the drawn back face toward you. Push the needle tip straight down through the monks cloth at the starting point of your first colour area. Push until the depth stop meets the cloth surface. Then withdraw the needle straight back up, lifting just clear of the cloth surface. Do not rotate the needle and do not pull it completely out of the weave; just clear the surface and move along one or two cloth threads. Repeat. Each push-and-withdraw cycle forms one loop on the front face.

The open slot on the side of the needle barrel must always face in the direction of travel as you work a row. If the slot faces backward or sideways, the yarn catches on the loop just formed and the pile becomes tangled. Maintain a steady rhythm: push, withdraw, move, push. Do not pull the yarn from behind; it feeds through automatically as the loops form.

Work the outline of each colour area first, then fill the interior. Outlines worked before fill give sharper edges between colours. Fill rows can run in any direction; diagonal rows produce a slightly different texture from straight horizontal or vertical rows, which can be used deliberately for botanical motifs to suggest leaf vein direction.

To change colour or start a new length, cut the yarn on the back face leaving a 3 cm tail. Thread the new yarn and bring it up through the same hole or the adjacent hole. Hold the tail on the back with a fingertip for the first two or three strokes of the new length to prevent it pulling through. Flip the work to check the front face regularly: check the pile height is even and that no rows have been skipped.
Step 4: Secure the back and lock the loops

Remove the finished cloth from the rug frame. Lay it pile-face down on a clean surface. Trim any yarn tails on the back to 1 cm. Apply a thin, even layer of latex or PVA fabric glue across the entire worked area on the back, spreading it with a piece of card or a brush to cover all the loop base-points without over-saturating the cloth. Allow to dry fully (at least one hour for latex glue; two to three hours for PVA). This step is non-optional for a wall piece: without the glue layer, loops can be pulled free from the back if the work is handled, framed, or transported.
Step 5: Back with felt and trim

Cut a piece of felt to match the 30 x 30 cm design area exactly. Apply a second thin layer of fabric glue to the dried back of the monks cloth, covering the same worked area. Press the felt firmly onto the glue layer, smoothing from the centre outward to avoid air pockets. Weight with a flat board or heavy books and allow to dry completely. The felt backing adds body, covers the glue layer, and gives the mounted piece a clean back face.

Once dry, trim the cloth and felt backing together to a circle roughly 30 to 32 cm in diameter, centred on the design. The exact diameter depends on the size of the display hoop: the cloth needs to reach just over the outer hoop ring when mounted.
Step 6: Mount in the display hoop

Loosen the 25 cm embroidery hoop fully. Place the inner ring on a flat surface. Lay the punch-needle piece pile-face up over the inner ring, centring the design. Press the outer ring down over the cloth to seat it. Tighten the screw gradually, pulling the cloth taut between tightening steps until the pile surface sits flat and evenly tensioned. The outer ring holds the monks cloth edge in place without stapling or stitching.

Trim the monks cloth overhang on the back to 3 cm all around. Fold the overhang flat against the back of the inner hoop ring and fix with a few stitches of strong thread, or apply a thin line of fabric glue to the inner face of the hoop and press the folded cloth against it. Allow to dry before hanging.
Common mistakes

items.problem: Loops pull free when the cloth is removed from the frame or handled
items.cause: The glue layer was applied too thinly, or was not allowed to dry fully before the felt backing was added.
items.fix: Apply a second layer of glue to the back, allow to dry completely, and press the felt again. If loops continue to pull through, the monks cloth weave may be too open: re-work those areas on a tighter backing and apply a heavier glue layer.

items.problem: The pile height is uneven: some rows sit noticeably higher or lower than adjacent rows
items.cause: The depth stop shifted during work, or the needle was not pushed fully to the stop on every stroke.
items.fix: Check the depth stop is locked firmly before each working session. Develop a consistent rhythm: push until resistance is felt at the stop, then lift. Rows that sit too low can be re-worked in the same holes; rows that sit too high can be trimmed flush with sharp scissors.

items.problem: Colour edges are blurry rather than sharp
items.cause: The outline row of each colour area was not worked before filling the interior, or outline rows were placed more than one cloth thread outside the drawn line.
items.fix: Work outlines first, directly on the drawn design line, before filling. Keep the needle perpendicular to the cloth on every stroke: a tilted needle places the loop up to 4 mm away from the intended position.

items.problem: The monks cloth puckers when mounted in the hoop
items.cause: The cloth was removed from the rug frame before the glue layer dried, allowing it to relax unevenly, or the design was drawn off-grain.
items.fix: Before mounting, dampen the back of the monks cloth lightly, lay the piece flat under a weight, and allow to dry. Then mount in the hoop, tightening gradually and pulling the edges taut between tightening steps.

content.text: Finishing and care

Hang the mounted piece on a picture hook or a nail using the hoop screw-mount as the hanging point. The screw housing at the top of the outer ring is strong enough for a piece of this size. If the hoop screw feels insecure, add a loop of strong thread knotted around the screw and fastened to the nail.

Dust the pile surface occasionally with a very soft brush, brushing lightly across the pile. Do not vacuum a wall-mounted punch-needle piece: the suction can disturb the loops. If the piece is taken down for storage, lay it flat rather than rolling or folding it, as rolling compresses the loops and can leave permanent creases in the monks cloth.
